text: Spatial transcriptomics (ST) has revolutionized our understanding of tissue architecture by coupling gene expression profiles with spatial coordinates. However, fully leveraging this data requires specialized computational tools capable of resolving tissue domains, integrating multiple samples, and transitioning from spot-level to cell-level resolution. Here, we present GraphST, a graph self-supervised contrastive learning framework designed for the comprehensive analysis of ST data. GraphST utilizes graph neural networks to capture spot-specific gene expression and spatial proximity, employing a contrastive learning strategy that refines latent representations by emphasizing local neighborhood contexts. Our method demonstrates superior performance in spatial clustering across diverse platforms—including 10x Visium, Stereo-seq, and Slide-seqV2—consistently outperforming existing tools in delineating fine-grained anatomical structures in brain and embryonic tissues. Furthermore, GraphST provides a robust solution for multi-sample integration, effectively correcting batch effects to align serial or adjacent sections. In the realm of cell-type deconvolution, GraphST utilizes an augmentation-free contrastive mechanism to project scRNA-seq data onto ST spots, enabling precise mapping of cell states and immune infiltrates in complex environments such as breast cancer tumors and lymph nodes. By integrating these three critical tasks into a single, efficient workflow, GraphST offers a powerful tool for deciphering the molecular and cellular organization of multicellular tissues.
